edges.description: Loss of turbinate tissue eliminates the structures that direct and regulate laminar airflow through the nasal cavity.
edges.source: Turbinate tissue loss from surgical resection

edges.description: Removal of turbinate mucosa reduces goblet cell density and disrupts the mucus blanket essential for mucociliary transport.
edges.source: Turbinate tissue loss from surgical resection

edges.description: Loss of seromucinous glands and mucosal surface area reduces the ability to warm, humidify, and filter inspired air.
edges.source: Turbinate tissue loss from surgical resection

edges.description: Surgical resection destroys or damages trigeminal sensory nerve fibers and TRPM8-expressing cool thermoreceptors in the turbinate mucosa.
edges.source: Turbinate tissue loss from surgical resection

edges.description: Inadequate sensory feedback from nasal airflow receptors generates a perception of obstruction and suffocation despite patent airways.
Reduced TRPM8-mediated cool sensing leads the brain to misinterpret adequate airflow as insufficient ventilation.

nodes.description: The turbinates normally create resistance and direct laminar airflow through the nasal cavity. Their removal results in abnormally wide nasal passages with turbulent airflow patterns and paradoxical redistribution of airflow away from the inferior meatus toward the middle meatus, reducing mucosal wall shear stress and sensory stimulation.

evidence.snippet: symptomatic ENS patients have paradoxical significantly less airflow in the inferior meatus

evidence.reference_title: Empty nose syndrome pathogenesis and cell-based biotechnology products as a new option for treatment.
evidence.snippet: ENS pathogenesis is multifactorial and includes changes in laminar physiological airflow, disruption of mucosa functions and deficient neural sensation.

nodes.description: Turbinate mucosa is rich in goblet cells that produce the mucus blanket essential for mucociliary clearance. Surgical removal of turbinate tissue reduces goblet cell density and disrupts the mucociliary escalator, impairing particle trapping and pathogen removal. Histopathology shows goblet cell metaplasia, indicating mucosal remodeling that alters normal secretory function and mucociliary transport.

evidence.reference_title: Distinct Histopathology Characteristics in Empty Nose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Additionally, a unique histological change called goblet cell metaplasia was found in the ENS group.

nodes.description: The nasal turbinates play a critical role in warming, humidifying, and filtering inspired air through their rich vascular supply and seromucinous glands. Surgical resection of turbinate tissue reduces the mucosal surface area and submucosal gland density available for conditioning inspired air, leading to mucosal dryness, crusting, and nasal discomfort. Histopathology demonstrates squamous metaplasia, submucosal fibrosis, and reduced submucosal gland number.

evidence.reference_title: Distinct Histopathology Characteristics in Empty Nose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Patients with ENS presented significantly more squamous metaplasia, a higher rate of submucosal fibrosis, and a lower submucosal gland number grading.

nodes.description: The central clinical paradox of ENS: patients with wide-open nasal passages experience subjective nasal obstruction and air hunger. This results from the convergence of disrupted airflow dynamics and trigeminal neurosensory dysfunction, where inadequate mucosal cooling and mechanosensory stimulation are interpreted by the brain as inadequate ventilation. The sensation of suffocation causes significant distress and psychological morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Empty nose syndrome pathogenesis and cell-based biotechnology products as a new option for treatment.
evidence.snippet: The main feature of ENS is paradoxical nasal obstruction feeling despite objectively wide nasal airway.
The most common clinical symptoms are paradoxical nasal obstruction, nasal dryness and crusting, and a persistent feeling of dyspnea.

evidence.reference_title: Empty nose syndrome: what are we really talking about?
evidence.snippet: The problem with empty nose syndrome is probably not that it does not exist, it is that we cannot adequately explain its existence by what we currently understand about the nose.

nodes.description: Loss of nasal airflow sensory feedback due to damage or downregulation of trigeminal cool thermoreceptors, particularly TRPM8 channels, in the remaining nasal mucosa. ENS patients demonstrate impaired menthol lateralization detection thresholds and significantly lower TRPM8 immunoexpression compared to controls, indicating peripheral neurosensory dysfunction that contributes to paradoxical obstruction.

evidence.reference_title: Distinct Histopathology Characteristics in Empty Nose Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The ENS group had a significantly lower expression level of TRPM8.
a combination of distorted nasal aerodynamics and loss of mucosal sensory function may potentially lead to ENS symptomology.

evidence.reference_title: Update on empty nose syndrome: disease mechanisms, diagnostic tools, and treatment strategies.
evidence.snippet: new data suggest that impaired trigeminal nerve function may also play a role in the pathophysiology of the disease.
Aberrations in neurosensory systems from improper healing may play a major role in the abnormal sensations ENS patients experience.

evidence.reference_title: Empty Nose Syndrome Pathophysiology: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: An abnormal trigeminal-thermoreceptor response may be present in some patients.

nodes.description: Surgical reduction or total resection of the nasal turbinates, most commonly the inferior turbinates and sometimes the middle turbinates, removes the mucosal and bony tissue that normally modulates nasal airflow. This can result from turbinectomy, aggressive turbinoplasty, or submucosal resection performed for chronic nasal obstruction or rhinitis.
nodes.id: Turbinate tissue loss from surgical resection

cell_types: Nasal cavity respiratory epithelium epithelial cell

evidence.snippet: Empty nose syndrome (ENS) is a rare, late complication of turbinate surgery.

evidence.reference_title: Surgical interventions for empty nose syndrome: A meta-analysis of meta-analyses.
evidence.snippet: Empty nose syndrome (ENS) is a complex iatrogenic condition resulting from excessive inferior turbinate resection, leading to paradoxical nasal obstruction despite an objectively patent airway.
